headline: Ethnic Profiling in Police Stops: Indians 5.8 Times More Likely to Be Stopped
context: A 2025 University of Malaya criminology study using observer methodology found Indian Malaysians are 5.8 times more likely to be stopped by police than their population share would predict.

cards.big: Indian Malaysians are 6.3% of the population.
cards.sub: They account for 36.6% of police stops in the UM study — 5.8 times their population share.

cards.big: 78% of Indian respondents reported being stopped without stated reason.
cards.sub: Malay respondents: 23%. Chinese respondents: 31%. The disparity is consistent across all study areas.

cards.big: PDRM has no published guidelines on stop-and-search ethnicity monitoring.
cards.sub: Without data collection, the disparity is invisible to the institution producing it.

cards.big: UK police are required to record ethnicity for every stop under PACE Act 1984.
cards.sub: Published data led to accountability reforms. Measurement enabled change.

cards.big: 5.8 times more likely to be stopped for being Indian. If this were a drug, it would be pulled from the market.
Ethnic profiling is measurable and correctable — but only if measured. PDRM should record and publish ethnicity data for all stops. Transparency is the prerequisite for accountability.
